problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem C (data): given five years of daily gold and bitcoin price streams, develop a model that produces an optimal DAILY portfolio investment strategy across three assets (cash = risk-free, gold and bitcoin = risky), explicitly accounting for per-trade transaction commissions (gold 1%, bitcoin 2%), then demonstrate the strategy is optimal and analyze sensitivity to transaction costs. The paper frames it as a Markowitz-style return-vs-risk portfolio problem solved day-by-day in a rolling loop over the 5-year horizon, reporting a 616.63% total / 261.41% average-annual return.
models_used: ARIMA(p,d,q) price-forecasting model — one-day-ahead forecasts of gold (G_t) and bitcoin (B_t); d=1 via first-order differencing, order (p,q) chosen by minimizing BIC over a programmed grid; validated with ADF/unit-root test for stationarity and Ljung-Box Q(m) white-noise residual test

Moving-Average 'long arrangement' (bull/bear regime) trading-day filter — 10 MAs (MA5,7,10,13,16,20,25,30,60,120); a near-complete long/short arrangement with >=8 of 10 slopes positive/negative flags bull/bear, producing binary trade-gate indicators M_t (gold) and N_t (bitcoin) that suppress trading in sideways markets to avoid commission churn

CVaR (Conditional Value-at-Risk) risk measure under a Gaussian-return assumption — closed-form CVaR = C(alpha)*sqrt(W^T C W) - W^T mu, using a rolling portfolio weight vector W_t, mean-return vector mu_t and covariance matrix C_t; chosen over variance/beta and plain VaR for sub-additivity, coherence and convexity

Bi-objective optimization: maximize expected daily profit Ĩ_t (return net of commissions) AND minimize CVaR, subject to no-short-sell (y_t,z_t>=0), no-borrow (x_t>=0), and the M_t/N_t regime gating constraints
NSGA-II (elitist fast non-dominated sorting genetic algorithm, Pareto-based) — solves the bi-objective program each day to yield a Pareto-feasible set of daily allocations [x_t,y_t,z_t]

Downside semi-variance secondary selector — from the Pareto set, compute downside semi-variance V = W^T C^- W, sort ascending, impose the 70% quantile as a daily risk cap, and pick the max-return allocation under that cap as the day's final trade; iterate day-by-day
Markowitz mean-variance portfolio theory as the conceptual backbone framing the whole return/risk tradeoff
key_techniques: Pipeline decomposition: forecast (ARIMA) -> regime gate (MA arrangement) -> risk model (CVaR) -> Pareto optimize (NSGA-II) -> tie-break (downside semi-variance 70% cap), run in a rolling daily loop — each module's output feeds the next as a clean interface (G_t,B_t; M_t,N_t; CVaR; feasible set; final allocation)

Commission-aware objective: transaction cost terms -1%|y_t-y_{t-1}|G_t and -2%|z_t-z_{t-1}|B_t are baked directly into both the profit objective and the cash constraint, so the optimizer internally trades off rebalancing benefit against cost

Regime filter as a churn-control device: explicitly NOT trading during sideways markets to keep cumulative commissions down — a domain-motivated way to cut transaction cost rather than a pure return-maximizer

Two-stage risk handling: CVaR enters as an optimization objective for the Pareto front, then a SEPARATE downside semi-variance quantile cap is used as the final risk-tolerance knob (asymmetric risk: penalize downside deviations only, leave upside unbounded) — this also doubles as the user-tunable risk-aversion parameter
Rigorous time-series model validation: stationarity via ACF decay + ADF unit-root test, order selection via BIC grid search, residual adequacy via Ljung-Box at 5% — textbook Box-Jenkins discipline
Multi-metric forecast accuracy reporting (MSE, RMSE, MAE, MAPE, R2) with MAPE<0.1 and R2>0.94 as headline credibility numbers

Two independent optimality arguments: (a) critical-period case studies showing buy-early-in-bull / exit-before-bear behavior, (b) Monte-Carlo random-interference test — 1000 perturbed strategies (±10% holdings), showing the chosen strategy beats ~93% of them

Sensitivity + robustness combined: sweep bitcoin cost {1,2,3%} x gold cost {0.5,1,1.5%}, quantify elasticity (bitcoin -1% cost -> +1.75% annual return), and re-run the 1000-perturbation ranking at each cost setting to show top-10% robustness
why_it_won: "It read as a complete, professionally-grounded quantitative-finance pipeline rather than a single model. Judges reward (1) correct method-to-task fit: ARIMA for short-memory price series, CVaR (a coherent, sub-additive risk measure) over naive variance, NSGA-II for a genuine two-objective return/risk tradeoff, and Markowitz/Rockafellar-Uryasev citations showing the team knew the canonical literature; (2) the transaction-cost requirement is treated as first-class — it appears in the objective, the constraints, AND gets a dedicated sensitivity section with an elasticity number, exactly what the prompt demanded; (3) the regime filter is an elegant, interpretable answer to the 'commission makes frequent trading bad' subtlety that many teams miss; (4) strong, layered validation (BIC, Ljung-Box, MAPE/R2, critical-period case studies, and a 1000-run Monte-Carlo that benchmarks the strategy against 93% of perturbations) substitutes a defensible optimality argument for an impossible ground-truth comparison; (5) the model is explicitly tunable to any trader's risk aversion via the semi-variance quantile cap, signaling generality. The writing is clean, the flowchart up front orients the reader, and the memo is concrete and trader-facing."
section_highlights: "Front-loaded mermaid/flowchart (Fig 1) gives judges the whole 5-stage pipeline at a glance before any math. Section 3 does real data hygiene (10 missing gold values explained as market-closed days, filled with prior close) plus descriptive stats (per-year std-dev and gold-bitcoin Pearson correlation) that motivate later modeling choices. Section 4 is the spine: each sub-model (4.1 ARIMA, 4.2 MA regime, 4.3 CVaR, 4.4 NSGA-II + semi-variance) follows a consistent 'principle -> build -> results analysis' template, which makes a complex pipeline easy to follow. Section 5 separates prediction accuracy (Table 2 MAPE/R2) from strategy effectiveness (critical-period bull/bear case studies + 1000-run random interference), a clean two-pronged optimality proof. Section 6 ties macro narrative (Trump election, Fed hikes, COVID) to a structured transaction-cost sweep (Tables 4-5) with explicit elasticities and top-10% robustness rankings. The two-page trader Memorandum restates model, robustness, sensitivity, and concrete year-by-year P&L in plain language — a strong deliverable-awareness signal."
pitfalls_or_limitations: The CVaR closed form assumes returns are Gaussian — bitcoin returns are famously heavy-tailed/skewed, so the risk model likely understates true tail risk; the paper concedes this only briefly in Weaknesses

ARIMA only makes a ONE-day-ahead forecast yet the optimizer's profit objective uses that forecast as if known — this risks look-ahead/over-optimistic returns; it is unclear whether the day-t decision strictly uses only information available through day t-1 (potential subtle data leakage inflating the 616% return)

Headline returns (261% avg annual, 664% peak year) are implausibly high for a real long-only 3-asset portfolio and are never benchmarked against a buy-and-hold or index baseline — the random-interference test only compares against ±10% perturbations of the SAME strategy, which is a weak optimality bar (it shows local, not global, optimality)

Several formulas are typographically broken/inconsistent in the manuscript (e.g. the semi-variance covariance σ_ij^{2-} expression, the variance formula missing a square), making exact reproduction hard
Magic numbers are unjustified: the >=8-of-10 MA-slope threshold, the 70% downside-semi-variance quantile cap, and the ±10% perturbation band are asserted without tuning/ablation
Tested on exactly one 5-year dataset with no out-of-sample/walk-forward split or cross-validation, so generalization is unproven (the paper admits this)
NSGA-II is stochastic and re-run daily over ~1800 days; no discussion of convergence, seed stability, or runtime cost beyond a vague 'running speed needs improvement'
reusable_patterns: Multi-stage pipeline architecture for data/optimization problems: predict -> filter/gate -> score risk -> Pareto-optimize -> tie-break, each stage a swappable module with a clean numeric interface; present it as a flowchart on page 1

Bake the problem's explicit cost/constraint (here: commissions) into BOTH the objective and the feasibility constraints, then give it its own sensitivity section with a quantified elasticity — directly satisfies prompt sub-requirements

Use a binary regime/gate indicator (M_t, N_t from MA arrangement) to suppress action in low-signal states — a clean, interpretable way to control cost/churn that generalizes to any 'act only when it's worth it' decision

Two-stage risk treatment: one risk measure as an optimization objective (CVaR on the Pareto front), a second as a tunable user-facing cap (downside semi-variance quantile) that encodes risk-aversion — lets you claim the model adapts to any user

Substitute a defensible optimality argument for missing ground truth: combine targeted case studies (critical periods) with a Monte-Carlo perturbation benchmark ('beats 93% of 1000 randomized variants')

Full Box-Jenkins validation checklist for any time-series component: ADF stationarity + ACF inspection, BIC order selection, Ljung-Box residual white-noise test, then MSE/RMSE/MAE/MAPE/R2 accuracy panel
Pair a macro/narrative context section with a structured parameter sweep so sensitivity analysis feels motivated rather than mechanical, and re-run the robustness benchmark at each parameter setting

Always include a plain-language stakeholder deliverable (the trader memo) that restates model, evidence, sensitivity, and concrete results — and consider adding a buy-and-hold baseline to make headline numbers credible (a fix this paper omitted)
